reference: GATE CS — Operating System / Processes and Threads

map.condition: Computing final semaphore value after P and V ops
Checking if parbegin/parend can express a precedence graph
Maximum processes in critical section simultaneously (binary semaphore)
map.use: \text{At most } 1 \text{ process in CS at any time}

map.condition: Counting semaphore initialized to N — max concurrent access
map.use: \text{Up to } N \text{ processes may proceed concurrently}
\text{flag}[i]=\text{true},\ \text{turn}=j \Rightarrow P_i \text{ waits until } P_j \text{ exits CS}

formulas.name: Semaphore Final Value
formulas.plain: S_final = S_init - (number of P ops) + (number of V ops)
formulas.whenToUse: After a series of P (wait/down) and V (signal/up) operations on a semaphore, compute the resulting value.

terms.means: initial semaphore value
total number of P (wait/down) operations performed
total number of V (signal/up) operations performed

formulas.trap: Do not confuse P with signal and V with wait — P decrements (wait/down), V increments (signal/up). A common GATE MCQ swaps these.

formulas.name: Binary Semaphore Range
formulas.whenToUse: When the semaphore is used purely for mutual exclusion (one process at a time in CS).

terms.means: CS is free; a process may enter
CS is occupied; arriving processes block

formulas.trap: A binary semaphore does NOT automatically prevent priority inversion or deadlock — it only guarantees at most one process in CS.

formulas.name: Counting Semaphore — Concurrent Access
formulas.latex: S_{\text{init}} = N \Rightarrow \text{at most } N \text{ processes may be inside the guarded section}
formulas.plain: Counting semaphore initialized to N allows up to N concurrent processes inside the section
formulas.whenToUse: When limiting access to a resource pool of size N (e.g., N database connections).

terms.means: number of identical resource instances

formulas.trap: The semaphore value can go negative if processes block — |negative value| = number of blocked processes.

formulas.name: Atomicity of P and V
formulas.latex: \text{P}(S):\; S \leftarrow S-1;\ \text{if } S < 0 \text{ then block} \qquad \text{V}(S):\; S \leftarrow S+1;\ \text{if } S \le 0 \text{ then wakeup}
formulas.plain: P(S): decrement S; if S < 0 block. V(S): increment S; if S <= 0 wake one waiting process. Both are atomic (indivisible).
formulas.whenToUse: When justifying why semaphore operations prevent race conditions by themselves.

terms.means: process is suspended and placed in the semaphore's wait queue
one blocked process is moved to ready queue

formulas.trap: Semaphore operations are atomic because they are implemented as OS kernel services (not user-space busy-wait by default).

formulas.name: Three Conditions for Valid CS Solution
formulas.plain: Any correct critical section solution must satisfy: (1) Mutual Exclusion, (2) Progress, (3) Bounded Waiting
formulas.whenToUse: When evaluating whether a proposed synchronization scheme is correct (very frequent GATE pattern).

terms.means: at most one process in CS at any time
if no process is in CS, a waiting process must eventually enter
a process waiting to enter CS is not starved indefinitely

formulas.trap: A solution that only provides Mutual Exclusion but not Progress (e.g., strict alternation when one process never wants to enter) fails.

formulas.name: Critical Region Definition
formulas.latex: \text{Critical Region} = \text{segment of code accessing shared data that must not be executed simultaneously by} \ge 2 \text{ processes}
formulas.plain: A critical region is a code segment accessing shared resources that must be executed by at most one process at a time.
formulas.whenToUse: When identifying or defining what constitutes a critical section in a concurrent program.

terms.means: variables, files, or hardware shared among concurrent processes

formulas.trap: A critical region is NOT defined by causing crashes or bugs — it is defined by shared resource access. Any shared-variable access is potentially a critical region even if it seems harmless.

formulas.name: Peterson's Two-Process Algorithm
formulas.plain: Process i sets flag[i]=true, turn=j, then waits while flag[j] is true and turn==j. After CS, sets flag[i]=false.
formulas.whenToUse: Software solution for two-process mutual exclusion without hardware support.

terms.means: true when process i wants to enter CS
whose turn it is to enter CS when both want to

formulas.trap: Peterson's algorithm satisfies all three conditions (ME, Progress, Bounded Waiting) for exactly 2 processes. It does NOT generalize directly to N processes.

formulas.name: Readers-Writers Problem — Shared Variables
formulas.plain: Classic shared state: read_count tracks active readers; mutex protects read_count updates; writing is a binary semaphore for writer exclusion.
formulas.whenToUse: When analyzing or writing reader-writer synchronization code with semaphores.

terms.means: number of processes currently reading
semaphore protecting read_count (initialized to 1)
semaphore ensuring writer exclusion (initialized to 1)

formulas.trap: In the readers-priority version, writers can starve. In the writers-priority version, readers can starve. GATE questions test which version is in use.

formulas.name: Producer-Consumer (Bounded Buffer) Semaphores
formulas.plain: mutex=1 (mutual exclusion on buffer), empty=N (free slots), full=0 (filled slots). Producer does P(empty),P(mutex),...,V(mutex),V(full). Consumer does P(full),P(mutex),...,V(mutex),V(empty).
formulas.whenToUse: When analyzing the classic bounded-buffer producer-consumer synchronization.

terms.means: total buffer capacity (number of slots)
semaphore counting available empty slots
semaphore counting filled slots

formulas.trap: Swapping P(mutex) and P(empty) in the producer (or P(mutex) and P(full) in the consumer) causes deadlock.

formulas.name: Precedence Graph Definition
formulas.latex: P_i \rightarrow P_j \Rightarrow P_i \text{ must complete before } P_j \text{ starts}
formulas.plain: A directed edge from P_i to P_j means P_i must finish before P_j begins. The graph must be a DAG.
formulas.whenToUse: When reading or drawing a precedence (task dependency) graph to determine valid execution orderings.

terms.means: P_i is a predecessor of P_j
Directed Acyclic Graph — no cycles allowed

formulas.trap: A cycle in the precedence graph implies a deadlock — no valid execution order exists.

formulas.name: Series-Parallel Graph — Parbegin/Parend Equivalence
formulas.latex: \text{Precedence graph expressible by parbegin/parend} \iff \text{graph is series-parallel}
formulas.plain: A set of processes can be implemented using only parbegin/parend if and only if its precedence graph is a series-parallel graph.
formulas.whenToUse: When asked which type of graph can be represented by nested parbegin/parend constructs.

terms.means: graph built by sequential and parallel composition of smaller series-parallel graphs
language construct that starts a group of parallel processes and waits for all to finish

formulas.trap: A general DAG (tree included only if it is series-parallel) may NOT be expressible with parbegin/parend — GATE 1990 classic asks this; answer is series-parallel, NOT just any DAG or tree.

formulas.name: Valid Execution Order — Topological Sort
formulas.latex: \text{Valid execution order} \Leftrightarrow \text{topological sort of the precedence DAG}
formulas.plain: Any topological ordering of the precedence DAG is a valid sequential execution order consistent with all dependencies.
formulas.whenToUse: When listing all valid orderings of concurrent processes with precedence constraints.

terms.means: linear ordering where P_i appears before P_j whenever P_i -> P_j

formulas.trap: The number of valid orderings can be large; GATE usually asks whether a SPECIFIC ordering is valid, which is checked by verifying each edge is respected.

formulas.name: IPC Mechanisms — Key Types
formulas.plain: IPC mechanisms include: Shared Memory, Message Passing, Pipes, Sockets, Signals
formulas.whenToUse: When classifying or selecting an IPC method for a given scenario.

terms.means: processes share a region of memory; fastest IPC
processes exchange messages via send/receive primitives
unidirectional byte-stream channel between related processes

formulas.trap: I/O redirection (< and >) changes stdin/stdout file descriptors; a pipe (|) connects stdout of one program to stdin of another — they are related but distinct concepts.

formulas.name: Pipe Properties
formulas.latex: \text{Pipe: unidirectional, FIFO, between related processes (parent-child or siblings)}
formulas.plain: A pipe is unidirectional (data flows one way), operates as a FIFO queue, and is typically between a parent process and its child (or siblings).
formulas.whenToUse: When distinguishing pipes from other IPC methods or when I/O redirection vs. pipe is tested.

terms.means: First-In First-Out — data read in the order written
a single pipe allows data to flow in only one direction

formulas.trap: A pipe connects two programs (one writes, one reads); I/O redirection connects a program to a FILE. A pipe is NOT the same as I/O redirection.

formulas.name: Dining Philosophers — Deadlock Condition
formulas.latex: \text{Deadlock if all } N \text{ philosophers hold left fork and wait for right fork}
formulas.plain: Deadlock occurs when every philosopher picks up exactly one fork (left) and waits for the other — circular wait with N=5 philosophers.
formulas.whenToUse: When analyzing deadlock in resource-sharing scenarios with circular dependencies.

terms.means: number of philosophers (= number of forks)

formulas.trap: Allowing at most N-1 philosophers to sit simultaneously, or using an asymmetric rule (odd picks left first, even picks right first), breaks circular wait.

formulas.name: Monitor — High-Level Synchronization
formulas.latex: \text{Monitor} = \{\text{shared data} + \text{procedures} + \text{condition variables}\},\; \text{at most 1 process active inside at any time}
formulas.plain: A monitor encapsulates shared data and procedures; only one process may be executing inside a monitor at any time. Condition variables allow wait() and signal().
formulas.whenToUse: When the question asks about high-level synchronization constructs vs. semaphores.

terms.means: allows a process inside a monitor to wait (releasing the monitor) and be signaled later
releases monitor lock and suspends the calling process
wakes one waiting process; if none, has no effect (unlike V on semaphore)

formulas.trap: Monitor signal() with no waiting process has NO effect (unlike semaphore V which increments the counter). This is a frequent GATE distinction.

formulas.name: Race Condition Definition
formulas.latex: \text{Race condition: outcome depends on relative scheduling/interleaving of concurrent processes}
formulas.plain: A race condition exists when multiple processes access shared data concurrently and the final result depends on the order of execution.
formulas.whenToUse: When identifying whether a given concurrent program has a correctness issue due to unsynchronized shared access.

terms.means: the specific order in which concurrent instructions are executed

formulas.trap: Race conditions are eliminated by synchronization (mutual exclusion over the critical section), NOT by making processes run faster.

formulas.name: Busy-Wait (Spinlock) vs. Block
formulas.latex: \text{Busy-wait: } \textbf{while}\;(S \le 0)\;\text{do nothing} \quad \text{Block: OS suspends process until } S > 0
formulas.plain: Busy-wait keeps CPU spinning until semaphore is available (wastes CPU). Blocking suspends the process (efficient for long waits).
formulas.whenToUse: When comparing spinlock and blocking semaphore implementations.

terms.means: loop that continuously checks the semaphore — CPU is not released
process is placed in a wait queue; CPU is freed for other work

formulas.trap: Busy-wait is preferred for VERY SHORT critical sections on multi-core systems (avoids context-switch overhead). For long waits, blocking is always better.
